talk_title: ZC3H18 truncating mutations drive ERV accumulation and melanomagenesis

speaker.name: Megan Inscho
speaker.affiliation: Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, Department of Medicine, Harvard Medical School

targets.dependency_context: melanoma and epithelial cancers with ZC3H18 loss

biomarkers.name: ERV RNA expression
biomarkers.finding: ZC3H18 truncating mutants show >5‑fold upregulation of ERV transcripts in melanoma cell lines and patient tumors

models.name: MITFA‑BRAF melanoma model
models.genotype_or_key_features: p53‑/‑, MITFA‑/‑, human BRAF V600E; ZC3H18 truncation introduced

models.name: human melanoma cell lines (e.g., A375)
models.genotype_or_key_features: ZC3H18 wild‑type or CRISPR‑edited truncation; ERV upregulation measured

MOA_summary: Truncating mutations in the RNA‑surveillance factor ZC3H18 act in a dominant‑negative manner, losing interaction with the NEXT complex while retaining RNA‑binding capacity, leading to cytoplasmic sequestration and stabilization of endogenous retroviral RNAs; these ERV transcripts are necessary and sufficient to accelerate melanomagenesis in zebrafish and human models.
key_findings: 35% of melanoma patients harbor mono‑ or biallelic loss of ZC3H18, and ~30% of all solid tumors show focal deletions of the locus.
ZC3H18 truncations increase ERV RNA levels >10‑fold in zebrafish melanoma and human melanoma cell lines compared with wild‑type controls.
CRISPR‑mediated knockout of ZC3H18 recapitulates the ERV up‑regulation, whereas heterozygous loss shows a modest effect, supporting a dominant‑negative mechanism.
Exogenous expression of the truncation mutant alone is sufficient to drive ERV accumulation and enhances tumor initiation in zebrafish.
Base‑scope FISH demonstrates cytoplasmic accumulation of ERV RNAs in ZC3H18‑mutant cells, indicating mislocalization away from nuclear decay machinery.
CRISPR‑interference targeting the up‑regulated ERV transcripts fully rescues the accelerated oncogenesis phenotype in zebrafish, proving ERV RNAs are necessary for ZC3H18‑driven tumorigenesis.
open_questions: What molecular features of ERV RNAs confer oncogenic activity beyond innate immune activation?
How does the ZC3H18 truncation specifically sequester ERV RNAs without affecting other NEXT substrates?

talk_title: Splicing‑regulated LAG‑3 intron removal enables conditional FOXO1 and IL‑12 expression to overcome T‑cell exhaustion

speaker.name: Omar Abdelwahab
speaker.affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Chair of Molecular Pharmacology, Attending Physician, Leukemia Service

targets.dependency_context: exhausted CD8+ T cells in melanoma

biomarkers.name: LAG‑3 surface protein
biomarkers.finding: LAG‑3 surface expression increases 3‑fold in exhausted tumor‑infiltrating CD8+ T cells versus peripheral blood

biomarkers.name: PD‑1
biomarkers.finding: PD‑1 expression reduced by ~40% in T cells with splicing‑regulated FOXO1 expression

biomarkers.name: Granzyme B
biomarkers.finding: Granzyme B+ CD8+ T cells increased from 12% to 28% after chronic stimulation with splicing‑regulated FOXO1

models.name: melanoma tumor‑derived CD8+ T cells (n=4 patients)
models.genotype_or_key_features: sorted exhausted vs non‑exhausted subsets; long‑read scRNA‑seq

models.name: NY‑ESO‑1 TCR‑engineered T cells
models.genotype_or_key_features: P2A‑FOXO1 or P2A‑IL12 constructs under LAG3 intron control

models.name: in vivo mouse melanoma model
models.genotype_or_key_features: tumor implantation with adoptive transfer of engineered T cells

MOA_summary: Exhausted CD8+ T cells splice out a retained intron in LAG3, enabling full‑length LAG‑3 protein; this splicing switch is harnessed by inserting a therapeutic cassette downstream of the intron, so that FOXO1 or IL‑12 is expressed only after intron removal in chronically stimulated T cells, thereby reprogramming exhausted T cells to a less exhausted, more cytotoxic phenotype and improving anti‑tumor activity.
readouts: flow cytometry (surface LAG‑3, PD‑1, TIM‑3, granzyme B, IL‑12)
tumor growth curves in vitro (rechallenge assay)
key_findings: A previously unannotated intron in LAG3 is retained in acutely stimulated CD8+ T cells but efficiently removed in exhausted tumor‑infiltrating T cells, correlating with a 4‑fold increase in LAG‑3 mRNA and protein.
Minigene reporter assays show that forced intron retention abolishes LAG‑3 protein production and GFP reporter expression, confirming NMD‑mediated loss.
CRISPR screening identifies MBNL1 as a positive regulator of intron removal; MBNL1 knock‑down maintains intron retention and reduces LAG‑3 surface expression.
Engineering a LAG3‑intron‑controlled FOXO1 cassette yields conditional FOXO1 expression only after chronic stimulation, decreasing PD‑1, TIM‑3, and LAG‑3 while increasing granzyme B and perforin.
In repeated tumor rechallenge assays, T cells bearing the splicing‑regulated FOXO1 construct achieve 60% tumor clearance versus 35% with constitutive FOXO1 and 10% with control T cells.
A splicing‑regulated IL‑12 construct produces IL‑12 and IFN‑γ exclusively in exhausted T cells, demonstrating temporal control of a toxic cytokine.
open_questions: Which additional RNA‑binding proteins cooperate with MBNL1 to regulate the LAG3 intron during exhaustion?
Can the splicing‑controlled payload strategy be generalized to other exhaustion‑associated checkpoints (e.g., TIM‑3, TIGIT)?
